00001| <!--

00003| -->
00004| 
00005| ## Summary (D.E.K.N.)
00006| 
00007| **Delta:** Tiered recovery with advisory-first principle: Auto-resume works for rate limits, auto-respawn risks duplicate work, auto-abandon loses progress. Best approach is resume + escalation to human.
00008| 
00009| **Evidence:** Ghost visibility decision shows reversibility matters (filter > delete). Existing `orch resume` works for manual recovery. Daemon auto-completion works because completion is low-risk (agent explicitly said done). Recovery is higher-risk (guessing).
00010| 
00011| **Knowledge:** Different failure modes need different recovery: rate limit → resume works; server restart → resume may work (session on disk); context exhaustion → resume fails; infinite loop → resume perpetuates. One-size-fits-all recovery is wrong.
00012| 
00013| **Next:** Implement tiered recovery in daemon poll loop: (1) detect stuck agents, (2) attempt resume with rate limiting, (3) surface in Needs Attention if resume fails.

00016| 
00017| ---
00018| 
00019| # Investigation: Design Stuck Agent Recovery Mechanism
00020| 
00021| **Question:** When OpenCode workers get stuck after server restart or rate limit, should we auto-resume (send continue message), auto-respawn (new session same context), auto-abandon + re-queue, or use a tiered approach?
00022| 
00023| **Started:** 2026-01-15
00024| **Updated:** 2026-01-15
00025| **Owner:** Agent og-work-design-stuck-agent-15jan-faa3
00026| **Phase:** Complete
00027| **Next Step:** None - design ready for implementation
00028| **Status:** Complete
00029| 
00030| ---
00031| 
00032| ## Findings
00033| 
00034| ### Finding 1: Existing Resume Mechanism is Manual and Works
00035| 
00036| **Evidence:** `orch resume` command exists in `cmd/orch/resume.go`. It can:
00037| - Resume by beads ID: `orch resume proj-123`
00038| - Resume by workspace: `orch resume --workspace meta-orch-xyz`
00039| - Resume by session: `orch resume --session ses_abc123`
00040| 
00041| The resume prompt tells the agent to re-read its SPAWN_CONTEXT.md and continue work.
00042| 
00043| **Source:** `cmd/orch/resume.go:90-100` (GenerateResumePrompt function)
00044| 
00045| **Significance:** The machinery for resume already exists. The question is whether to automate it.
00046| 
00047| ---
00048| 
00049| ### Finding 2: Stalled Detection Already Designed (Advisory-Only)
00050| 
00051| **Evidence:** Investigation `.kb/investigations/2026-01-08-inv-design-stalled-agent-detection-agents.md` designed phase-based stalled detection:
00052| - Signal: Phase unchanged for 15+ minutes
00053| - Action: Surface in Needs Attention (advisory only)
00054| - No auto-abandon, no auto-restart
00055| - Single threshold (15 min), single signal (phase unchanged)
00056| 
00057| The design explicitly rejected auto-actions due to complexity spiral risk (Dec 27-Jan 2 rollback).
00058| 
00059| **Source:** `.kb/investigations/2026-01-08-inv-design-stalled-agent-detection-agents.md:44-49` (Success Criteria: "Advisory only - Surface in Needs Attention, don't auto-abandon")
00060| 
00061| **Significance:** Prior investigation established "advisory over automatic" as the right approach. Recovery design should follow this pattern.
00062| 
00063| ---
00064| 
00065| ### Finding 3: Ghost Visibility Decision Shows Reversibility Matters
00066| 
00067| **Evidence:** Decision `.kb/decisions/2026-01-15-ghost-visibility-over-cleanup.md` chose filtering over cleanup:
00068| - "Ghosts can exist indefinitely. The fix is how we count and display them, not whether they exist."
00069| - "Reversible? Yes (--all shows everything) vs No (deleted = gone)"
00070| 
00071| | Aspect | Cleanup | Filtering |
00072| |--------|---------|-----------|
00073| | Reversible? | No | Yes |
00074| | Edge cases | Many | Few |
00075| | Fights architecture? | Yes | No |
00076| 
00077| **Source:** `.kb/decisions/2026-01-15-ghost-visibility-over-cleanup.md:44-52`
00078| 
00079| **Significance:** Auto-respawn and auto-abandon are destructive (irreversible). Following the same logic, recovery should prefer non-destructive actions (resume, surface) over destructive ones (respawn, abandon).
00080| 
00081| ---
00082| 
00083| ### Finding 4: Different Failure Modes Need Different Recovery
00084| 
00085| **Evidence:** Analysis of failure modes reveals divergent recovery needs:
00086| 
00087| | Failure Mode | Session State | Recovery Strategy | Why |
00088| |--------------|---------------|-------------------|-----|
00089| | **Rate limit** | Valid, paused | Resume works | Just waiting for rate limit to clear |
00090| | **Server restart** | On-disk, not in memory | Resume may work | Session loads from disk on first message |
00091| | **Context exhaustion** | Valid but depleted | Resume fails | Agent can't process more tokens |
00092| | **Infinite loop** | Valid but stuck | Resume perpetuates | Bad state is persistent |
00093| | **Crash** | Lost | Respawn needed | No session to resume |
00094| 
00095| **Source:** Derived from `.kb/guides/agent-lifecycle.md` (four-layer state model) and daemon operation model
00096| 
00097| **Significance:** One-size-fits-all recovery is wrong. A tiered approach that tries the cheapest action first and escalates on failure is the right architecture.
00098| 
00099| ---
00100| 
00101| ### Finding 5: Daemon Already Has Completion Loop Architecture
00102| 
00103| **Evidence:** Daemon has separate completion loop (every 60s) that:
00104| 1. Polls for Phase: Complete comments
00105| 2. Verifies completion (check artifacts)
00106| 3. Closes beads issues
00107| 4. Releases pool slots
00108| 
00109| This is parallel to the spawn loop, not blocking it.
00110| 
00111| **Source:** `.kb/guides/daemon.md:259-299` (Completion Detection section), `.kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation.md:46-58`
00112| 
00113| **Significance:** Recovery could fit into this architecture as a third loop: "Check for stuck agents → Attempt resume → Surface if stuck persists". The daemon is the right place for automated recovery.
00114| 
00115| ---
00116| 
00117| ### Finding 6: Principles Constrain Recovery Design
00118| 
00119| **Evidence:** Relevant principles from `~/.kb/principles.md`:
00120| 
00121| 1. **Gate Over Remind** (but gates must be passable): Recovery should be automatic where safe, but destructive actions need human decision.
00122| 
00123| 2. **Verification Bottleneck**: "The system cannot change faster than a human can verify behavior." Auto-respawn would create new sessions faster than humans can verify the original session's state.
00124| 
00125| 3. **Session Amnesia**: Agents forget between sessions. Resume prompt must provide context. Current resume prompts point back to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md (good).
00126| 
00127| 4. **Friction is Signal**: Stuck agents are friction - capture why they got stuck, don't just restart them.
00128| 
00129| **Source:** `~/.kb/principles.md` (Gate Over Remind:166-183, Verification Bottleneck:293-326, Session Amnesia:45-68, Friction is Signal:493-539)
00130| 
00131| **Significance:** Recovery automation has limits. Destructive recovery (respawn, abandon) requires human verification. Non-destructive recovery (resume) can be automated with rate limiting.
00132| 
00133| ---
00134| 
00135| ## Synthesis
00136| 
00137| **Key Insights:**
00138| 
00139| 1. **Advisory-First is the Pattern** - Both stalled detection (Jan 8) and ghost visibility (Jan 15) chose advisory/visibility over automatic action. Recovery should follow this pattern: try non-destructive resume, then surface for human decision.
00140| 
00141| 2. **Failure Mode Determines Recovery** - Rate limits recover via resume. Server restarts might recover via resume. Context exhaustion and infinite loops don't recover via resume. The system can't distinguish these reliably, so it must try resume first and escalate on failure.
00142| 
00143| 3. **Daemon is the Right Location** - Recovery fits naturally into the daemon's poll-based architecture. A recovery loop parallel to spawn and completion loops provides the automation while keeping the system simple.
00144| 
00145| **Answer to Investigation Question:**
00146| 
00147| **Use a tiered approach** with advisory-first principle:
00148| 
00149| | Tier | Action | Condition | Destructive? | Automatic? |
00150| |------|--------|-----------|--------------|------------|
00151| | 1 | Resume | Idle >10min, no Phase: Complete | No | Yes (rate-limited) |
00152| | 2 | Surface | Resume didn't help after 15min | No | Yes (visibility) |
00153| | 3 | Human decision | Surfaced agent | Varies | No |
00154| 
00155| **Why not other options:**
00156| 
00157| - **Auto-resume only**: Doesn't handle cases where resume fails (context exhaustion, infinite loops)
00158| - **Auto-respawn**: Irreversible, risks duplicate work if original session has partial commits
00159| - **Auto-abandon + re-queue**: Irreversible, loses any uncommitted progress
00160| - **No automation**: Manual resume works but doesn't scale; stuck agents accumulate
00161| 
00162| ---
00163| 
00164| ## Structured Uncertainty
00165| 
00166| **What's tested:**
00167| 
00168| - ✅ `orch resume` sends continuation prompt (verified: read resume.go source, command exists)
00169| - ✅ Daemon completion loop operates parallel to spawn loop (verified: daemon.md guide)
00170| - ✅ Ghost visibility filtering implemented for concurrency and display (verified: decision document)
00171| 
00172| **What's untested:**
00173| 
00174| - ⚠️ 10-minute threshold for stuck detection (educated guess, may need tuning)
00175| - ⚠️ Resume success rate for different failure modes (unknown without production data)
00176| - ⚠️ Rate limiting (1 resume/hour) is the right frequency (untested)
00177| 
00178| **What would change this:**
00179| 
00180| - If resume has >90% success rate → could be more aggressive with auto-resume
00181| - If resume causes problems (infinite loops worse after resume) → need smarter detection
00182| - If stuck agents are rare (<5%) → manual resume may be sufficient
00183| 
00184| ---
00185| 
00186| ## Implementation Recommendations
00187| 
00188| **Purpose:** Bridge from investigation findings to actionable implementation using directive guidance pattern.
00189| 
00190| ### Recommended Approach: Tiered Recovery with Advisory First
00191| 
00192| **Why this approach:**
00193| - Follows established patterns (advisory over automatic)
00194| - Non-destructive first (resume), escalate to human for destructive
00195| - Fits into existing daemon architecture
00196| - Preserves reversibility (no session deletion)
00197| 
00198| **Trade-offs accepted:**
00199| - Some stuck agents won't recover (context exhaustion, infinite loops)
00200| - Human intervention still required for complex cases
00201| - ~15 minute latency before surfacing in dashboard
00202| 
00203| **Implementation sequence:**
00204| 1. **Stuck detection** - Add to daemon poll loop: identify agents idle >10min without Phase: Complete
00205| 2. **Auto-resume** - Attempt `orch resume` for stuck agents (rate-limited: 1/hour per agent)
00206| 3. **Surface in dashboard** - If still stuck after resume + 15min, add to Needs Attention
00207| 
00208| ### Alternative Approaches Considered
00209| 
00210| **Option B: Full Automation (auto-resume → auto-respawn)**
00211| - **Pros:** No human intervention needed, handles all failure modes
00212| - **Cons:** Auto-respawn risks duplicate work, irreversible, violates reversibility principle
00213| - **When to use instead:** If stuck agents become >30% of spawns and human capacity is limited
00214| 
00215| **Option C: Manual Only (visibility + dashboard)**
00216| - **Pros:** Simplest, no automation risk, human always decides
00217| - **Cons:** Doesn't scale, stuck agents accumulate, requires constant monitoring
00218| - **When to use instead:** If stuck agents are <5% and automation causes problems
00219| 
00220| **Rationale for Tier 1 Recommendation:** Option A (tiered with advisory-first) provides automation where safe (resume is non-destructive) while preserving human decision for destructive actions (respawn, abandon). This matches established patterns from stalled detection and ghost visibility decisions.
00221| 
00222| ---
00223| 
00224| ### Implementation Details
00225| 
00226| **What to implement first:**
00227| 1. Stuck detection in daemon (highest impact, enables everything else)
00228| 2. Resume tracking (prevent infinite resume loops)
00229| 3. Needs Attention integration (surface stuck agents)
00230| 
00231| **Things to watch out for:**
00232| - ⚠️ Resume may wake agents in bad state (infinite loop) - limit to 1 resume/hour
00233| - ⚠️ Server restart may invalidate all sessions temporarily - don't mass-resume immediately
00234| - ⚠️ Rate limit recovery may need delay (wait for rate limit to clear before resume)

### Finding 4: Recovery Infrastructure 95% Complete

**Evidence:** Code review shows `RunPeriodicRecovery()` (lines 1086-1173), `ShouldRunRecovery()` (lines 1063-1074), config fields (lines 69-83), and helper functions (recovery.go) all exist. The `resumeAttempts` map is initialized in `NewWithConfig()` (line 212) for rate limiting. Missing: (1) Call to `RunPeriodicRecovery()` in daemon loop, (2) `resumeAttempts` init in `NewWithPool()`, (3) Recovery status output.

**Source:** `pkg/daemon/daemon.go:69-83, 104-107, 212, 1063-1173`, `pkg/daemon/recovery.go:28-176`

**Significance:** The tiered recovery is already built - we just need to wire it into the main daemon loop. This is a ~10 line addition vs. implementing from scratch.

---

## Synthesis

**Key Insights:**

1. **Recovery Infrastructure Already Exists** - Previous agent implemented the full recovery mechanism (detection, rate limiting, resume attempts) but didn't integrate it into the daemon loop. This reduces implementation to 3 small changes: (1) call RunPeriodicRecovery in loop, (2) fix NewWithPool init, (3) add status output.

2. **Stalled Detection is Independent** - The dashboard already has 15-minute stalled detection (serve_agents.go:332-341) that surfaces in Needs Attention. Recovery tries to prevent agents from hitting the stalled threshold by auto-resuming at 10 minutes. The two systems complement each other: recovery catches recoverable agents early, stalled detection catches unrecoverable ones.

3. **Rate Limiting Prevents Infinite Loops** - The `resumeAttempts` map tracks last resume time per agent (daemon.go:183-185). RunPeriodicRecovery checks this before resuming (lines 1127-1138) to prevent spamming stuck agents. This implements the "1 resume per hour per agent" requirement from the design.

**Answer to Investigation Question:**

The tiered stuck agent recovery is implemented by adding RunPeriodicRecovery to the daemon loop (alongside reflection and cleanup). The recovery loop runs every 5 minutes, detects agents idle >10 minutes without Phase: Complete, attempts resume with 1-hour rate limiting, and relies on existing stalled detection (15min threshold) to surface agents that don't recover. Changes needed: (1) Call RunPeriodicRecovery in cmd/orch/daemon.go around line 302, (2) Initialize resumeAttempts in NewWithPool, (3) Add recovery config output to startup message.
